text:
MRI THORACIC AND LUMBAR SPINE WITH AND WITHOUT CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION: 71-year-old male with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (Gleason 9, germline BRCA2 mutation). New bone metastases identified on recent bone scan involving T10 and T12 vertebral bodies. Patient presenting with new thoracic back pain radiating around right chest wall (grade 2). Evaluate for epidural disease and/or spinal cord compression. Previously treated with ADT/abiraterone then enzalutamide; now biochemically and radiographically progressing.

TECHNIQUE: MRI of the thoracic and lumbar spine was performed on a 3.0 Tesla magnet. Sequences obtained: sagittal T1, sagittal T2, sagittal STIR, axial T1, axial T2, and axial T1 fat-saturated post-contrast (gadolinium) sequences through the thoracic and lumbar spine. 0.1 mmol/kg gadolinium-based contrast agent administered without adverse reaction.

COMPARISON: Technetium-99m bone scan performed approximately 2 weeks prior. CT chest/abdomen/pelvis from approximately 5 months prior.

FINDINGS:

THORACIC SPINE:

T10 Vertebral Body: There is a large, expansile osseous metastatic lesion involving the T10 vertebral body with replacement of greater than 70% of the normal marrow signal by T1 hypointense, T2 heterogeneously hyperintense tumor. The lesion demonstrates avid homogeneous gadolinium enhancement. The posterior cortex of the T10 vertebral body is disrupted with associated epidural soft tissue extension. The epidural tumor component measures approximately 8 mm in the anteroposterior dimension and extends craniocaudally over approximately 1.5 vertebral levels. The epidural soft tissue causes mass effect on the ventral thecal sac with an estimated 30-35% reduction in the anteroposterior diameter of the spinal canal at the T10 level. The spinal cord at this level demonstrates no intrinsic T2 signal abnormality. No cord edema or myelomalacic change identified. The cord appears slightly displaced posteriorly but maintains normal caliber and signal intensity on all sequences. Posterior elements (pedicles bilaterally, lamina, spinous process) show abnormal marrow signal consistent with osseous metastatic involvement, though cortical integrity of the pedicles appears grossly intact.

T12 Vertebral Body: Osseous metastatic lesion involving the T12 vertebral body with diffuse marrow replacement, T1 hypointense and STIR hyperintense, with avid post-contrast enhancement. No posterior cortical disruption identified. No epidural soft tissue extension. No thecal sac compromise. Posterior elements appear uninvolved.

T8 Vertebral Body: Small focus of T1 hypointensity and STIR hyperintensity in the right posterior aspect of the T8 vertebral body, enhancing on post-contrast sequences, measuring approximately 9 mm, compatible with a smaller osseous metastasis. Not appreciable on prior bone scan (below scan resolution threshold).

Other thoracic levels: No additional marrow signal abnormalities identified at the remaining thoracic vertebral levels (T1-T7, T9, T11). Intervertebral discs throughout the thoracic spine appear intact without loss of height or signal. Thoracic cord traverses the entire thoracic canal without additional points of compression.

Right 4th Rib: Expansile osseous lesion involving the posterior aspect of the right 4th rib with cortical thinning. The lesion measures approximately 2.6 cm in greatest dimension, T1 hypointense and STIR hyperintense with heterogeneous enhancement. No extraosseous soft tissue mass. No pleural involvement identified on the visible lung bases.

LUMBAR SPINE:

L2 Vertebral Body: Known metastatic lesion with diffuse marrow replacement, T1 hypointense, STIR hyperintense, post-contrast enhancing. Loss of approximately 15% of normal vertebral body height noted compared to adjacent levels, likely chronic/prior compression component. No significant retropulsion. No epidural extension. Posterior elements: bilateral pedicle signal abnormality consistent with metastatic involvement.

L3 Vertebral Body: Osseous metastatic lesion with patchy marrow infiltration, most conspicuous in the right hemi-vertebral body. No cortical disruption. No epidural component. Moderate-to-severe central canal stenosis at L3-L4 is present from degenerative facet hypertrophy and ligamentum flavum thickening — this appears chronic and is not attributable to neoplastic disease. No cauda equina compression.

L4 Vertebral Body: Diffuse marrow signal abnormality consistent with metastatic involvement. No posterior cortical disruption or epidural extension. Pedicles bilaterally demonstrate abnormal signal.

L1 and L5: No convincing marrow signal abnormality to suggest metastatic involvement at these levels. L5-S1 disc space shows moderate height loss and endplate degenerative changes, chronic appearance.

SACRUM AND PELVIS (partially visualized): Heterogeneous marrow signal throughout the sacrum and bilateral iliac crests visible at the superior margin of the lumbar sequences, consistent with extensive osseous metastatic disease previously characterized on bone scan and CT. No visible sacral nerve root involvement.

SPINAL CORD AND CONUS: The conus medullaris terminates at the L1-L2 level, which is normal in position. No intrinsic cord signal abnormality identified throughout the thoracic cord or conus. The cauda equina nerve roots in the lumbar canal appear intact bilaterally without thickening, clumping, or enhancement to suggest leptomeningeal involvement.

PARAVERTEBRAL SOFT TISSUES: No discrete paravertebral soft tissue masses identified. Mild paraspinal muscular edema in the mid-thoracic region, consistent with reactive changes in the setting of adjacent osseous disease. Bilateral paraspinal muscles show mild atrophy, as expected for patient age.

IMPRESSION:

1. Epidural tumor extension at T10 arising from the metastatic vertebral body lesion, with mass effect on the ventral thecal sac causing moderate narrowing of the spinal canal (approximately one-third reduction in the anteroposterior diameter). The spinal cord at T10 is displaced but shows no signal abnormality — no cord edema or myelomalacia identified at this time. This warrants prompt attention given the proximity to cord compromise.

2. Additional thoracic spine osseous metastatic lesions at T8 (newly identified, small) and T12 (known), the latter without epidural involvement.

3. Known lumbar spine osseous metastatic disease at L2, L3, and L4 without epidural soft tissue extension or evidence of cord/cauda equina involvement.

4. Right posterior 4th rib metastatic lesion (previously noted on bone scan), without associated soft tissue mass or pleural involvement.

5. Degenerative lumbar facet and ligamentous disease at L3-L4 producing moderate central stenosis, appearing chronic and unrelated to the neoplastic process.

6. No leptomeningeal enhancement identified.

Findings communicated to the referring oncology team at the time of interpretation given the epidural disease at T10 and the potential for neurologic deterioration if untreated.
